retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Antinuclear Antibodies (ANA) -- Testing
retrieved_docs.content: IIF patterns correlate to specific ANA subtypes, and pattern recognition is a valuable tool in ANA testing. Homogenous fluorescence pattern typically suggests antibodies directed at dsDNA, histones, or nucleosomes. [1] A membranous pattern may show antibodies to membrane proteins. Antibodies directed to other nuclear antigens correlate with speckled fluorescent patterns. Anti-Smith antibodies fluoresce in a course-speckled pattern. Anti-SSA/Ro and anti-SSB/La form a fine-speckled pattern. Discrete speckles represent antibodies targeted to the centromeres in cells undergoing interphase. Nucleolar speckles are associated with antibodies directed at DNA topoisomerase (Scl-70). The speckled cytoplasmic pattern suggests antibodies to aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase (Jo-1). [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Several patterns of ANAs have been reported, including speckled, homogenous, centromere, cytoplasmic, nucleolar, and dense fine speckled patterns. With the availability of more specific ANAs targeting specific antigens, the staining patterns of ANAs are not considered significant enough by themselves. ANAs with a dense, fine speckled pattern (anti-DFS70) are considered least pathological, and patients with ANAs with this pattern rarely develop systemic autoimmune diseases. The speckled pattern is seen when ANAs are directed against the antigens such as SSA, SSB, Smith, ribonucleoprotein. The homogenous pattern is associated with ANAs targeted at histones, chromatin, and dsDNA, while the centromere pattern is associated with Anti-centromere antibodies seen in limited systemic sclerosis. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Iritis -- Evaluation -- Antinuclear Antibody
retrieved_docs.content: Antinuclear antibody (ANA) is the most important marker for juvenile idiopathic arthritis in children. ANA positivity is an indicator of CAU. [91]

retrieved_docs.title: Undifferentiated Connective Tissue Disease -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis of undifferentiated connective tissue disease usually relies on the degree of organ involvement. However, up to 60% of cases will remain undifferentiated, and others may progress into a defined connective tissue disease. A couple of factors may help in the prediction of this progression, for instance, the presence of cytopenias at the time of diagnosis, the degree of changes of the nail fold-capillaroscopy test during the follow-up sessions, and the antinuclear antibody titers, all of which may prove predictive about the disease in the future. [23] [29]

retrieved_docs.title: Mixed Connective Tissue Disease -- Evaluation -- Laboratory Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Immunological markers include high titer speckled pattern anti-nuclear antibody (typically >1280) and high titer anti-U1-RNP. The sensitivity of the U1-RNP antibody is 100%, but its specificity is lower. Another potential marker for MCTD is the presence of survival of motor neuron (SMN) antibody directed to SMN and its complex. In a study comparing the prevalence of anti-SMN complex antibodies between patients with systemic lupus erythematosus and MCTD, the antibodies were present in 40% of MCTD patients, whereas only 10% of patients with systemic lupus erythematosus had the antibody. In this study, the estimated specificity of the anti-SMN antibody was 91.2% for MCTD. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Antinuclear Antibodies (ANA) -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Given that ANAs are present in up to 30% of the average healthy population, there are inherent challenges against using them to diagnose autoimmune connective tissue disorders. Positive results must be interpreted with the existing clinical manifestations to establish a diagnosis. Furthermore, initial immunofluorescence testing on HEp-2 (human epithelial laryngeal carcinoma type 2) cells subjectively depends on multiple factors, including the laboratory manufacturing the substrate cells, the skill of the individual reading the result, and the definition of a positive result in each laboratory. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Antinuclear Antibodies (ANA) -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic autoimmune disorders affect 3-5% of the general population, and ANAs are one of the few specific disease markers in their diagnosis. [16] Therefore, ANA testing is often the first step in diagnosing systemic autoimmune connective tissue disorders. Laboratories report the staining pattern and the titer of ANA as an indication for further testing. The presence of ANAs and their subtypes increase the likelihood of a systemic autoimmune disorder, and there are notable clinical correlations between the ANA subtypes and autoimmune connective tissue disorders. However, they do not necessarily confirm that an individual has or will develop an autoimmune disease. Although positive ANA results help diagnose several autoimmune disorders, the negative ANA titer is also expected in some specific inflammatory conditions, for instance, ankylosing spondylitis. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Antinuclear Antibodies (ANA) -- Clinical Significance -- Scleroderma
retrieved_docs.content: Scleroderma, or systemic sclerosis, involves progressive fibrosis of the skin and organs. Scleroderma presents either as a limited form or a diffuse cutaneous form. The diagnostic basis is a combination of clinical symptoms and increased ANA titers. Scl-70 is highly correlated with scleroderma, while anti-centromere antibodies are moderately correlated. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A positive ANA shall be followed by testing for more specific autoantibodies to detect the antigen responsible for the positive ANA. It must be noted that frequently, a positive ANA will not be associated with any of the known more specific autoantibodies. There are several possible targets for ANAs, with any peptide synthesized inside the cell's nucleus serving as a potential antigen. However, only a few have been identified as having clinical relevance so far. A positive ANA with negative testing for more specific autoantibody testing is less likely to be associated with systemic autoimmune disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Antinuclear Antibodies (ANA) -- Testing
retrieved_docs.content: While IIF-ANA remains the primary initial testing method, other methods, including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) and immunoassays, offer confirmatory testing for specific ANAs. ELISA offers quantitative screening for specific ANAs and has proven to perform comparably to IIF, and commercial panels for antibodies to SSA/Ro, SSB/La, Sm, Scl-70, Jo-1, and centromeres are available. [17] In addition, multiplex immunoassays offer similar benefits and use a series of known antigen-coated beads, and when combined with patient serum, indicate their specific antibody targets. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Undifferentiated Connective Tissue Disease -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Positive serological markers are considered essential in the diagnostic criteria for undifferentiated connective tissue disease, so the laboratory measures are beneficial in diagnosis, especially anti-Ro/SSA and anti-U1-RNP. Other tests helpful in diagnosis include routine screening tests, which include: complete blood count, C-reactive protein (CRP), erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), serum creatinine, urinalysis with microscopic analysis, rheumatoid factor (RF), and antinuclear antibodies (ANA). [7] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Antinuclear Antibodies (ANA) -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Antinuclear antibodies (ANA) refer to an autoantibody directed at material within the nucleus of a cell. ANAs are typically classified into two groups, antibodies to DNA and histones and antibodies to nuclear material. Antibodies to DNA and histones include anti-dsDNA antibodies and anti-histone antibodies. The remaining category includes an additional targeted nuclear antigen. The first to be identified in this category was the anti-Smith antibody. [3] Others include anti-SSA/Ro, anti-SSB/La, anti-U3-RNP, anticentromere, Scl-70, and Jo-1. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Antinuclear antibodies (ANA) are the hallmark of the disease and shall be the initial test performed. Immunofluorescence assay is considered the gold standard test for ANA. Although other detection methods such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and multiplex assays are widely available, they lack sensitivity. A positive ANA is seen in more than 97% of cases of SLE. However, it can also be seen in several other disorders and a significant proportion of the healthy population, and have a specificity of only 20%. Hence, a positive ANA does not confirm SLE diagnosis, but a negative ANA makes it significantly less likely. ANA negative SLE has been rarely described, although it is primarily due to methodical error. Those cases have either a positive ANA on immunofluorescence or a positive Anti-Ro (SSA) antibody.

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatopathology Evaluation of Tumors -- Clinical Significance -- Melanocytomas
retrieved_docs.content: The hallmark cells of BAP1-inactivated melanocytomas are large epithelioid melanocytes with well-defined cytoplasmic borders, abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, and vesicular nuclei with prominent nucleoli. These melanocytes, typically unpigmented, reside within the dermis and exhibit varying degrees of nuclear and nucleolar size variation, including nuclear pleomorphism with dispersed chromatin patterns. [29] Mitotic activity is low, and tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes are often observed. Loss of nuclear BAP1 expression, as detected by IHC, is diagnostically equivalent to biallelic BAP1 inactivation on molecular testing and can confirm the diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Antinuclear Antibodies (ANA) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The antinuclear antibody (ANA) is a defining feature of autoimmune connective tissue disease. ANAs are a class of antibodies that bind to cellular components in the nucleus, including proteins, DNA, RNA, and nucleic acid-protein complexes. [1] First described in 1948, ANA identification has been the foundation of diagnosis for autoimmune connective tissue disease, including systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), Sjogren's syndrome, and polymyositis/dermatomyositis. [2] Although 20 to 30% of the average population has detectable levels of ANAs, increased titers are characteristic of individuals with connective tissue disorders. [3] Thus, the sensitivity and specificity of the methods used to detect ANAs are crucial to diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Mediastinal Mass -- Evaluation -- Tissue Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Histopathological examination: Histopathological analysis of biopsy specimens is essential for establishing a definitive diagnosis. Histological features observed on microscopic examination, such as cellular morphology, tissue architecture, mitotic activity, and necrosis, provide valuable information about the nature of the mass. Immunohistochemical staining may be employed to characterize cellular markers further and differentiate between different tumor subtypes or to confirm the presence of specific antigens associated with certain diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is a finding of disease and is categorized as cancer; other and unspecified primary, indicating that the presence of malignant cellular infiltrate reflects an underlying malignant neoplasm of unknown primary origin. Recognizing this finding is essential for guiding further diagnostic work‑up and management of patients with cancers where the primary site cannot be identified.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1803
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings” includes specific abnormal results such as a positive culture (المزرعة إيجابية) and an abnormal chromosome analysis (تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي). These particular findings are considered sub‑categories of the broader group of nonspecific abnormal laboratory results, indicating that when such tests return these outcomes, they represent distinct types of abnormal cytological, histological, immunological, or DNA evidence that require further clinical interpretation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 237
retrieved_docs.content: positivity intersect with these gastrointestinal pathologies. Recognizing these interconnections supports comprehensive assessment, diagnosis, and management of patients presenting with overlapping gastrointestinal and hematologic findings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is closely linked to داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيبي, an anti‑basement membrane disease of the kidney, and it also forms a key component of متلازمة رئية كلوية, a pulmonary‑renal syndrome that involves simultaneous injury to the lungs and kidneys. This interrelationship highlights the importance of recognizing necrotizing glomerulonephritis not only as a severe renal pathology but also as a manifestation of systemic autoimmune processes that can affect multiple organ systems, guiding comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and coordinated therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 585
retrieved_docs.content: Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings encompass a broad spectrum of atypical laboratory results, and within this category a positive culture (المزرعة إيجابية) is identified as a specific child finding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2623
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic Lesion is recognized as a diagnostic finding associated with سرطانة العظم, indicating that the disease has spread beyond its primary site. In the context of سرطانة العظم, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as the abnormal cellular component that drives the malignancy. Together, the presence of malignant tumor cells and the detection of metastatic lesions provide critical information for diagnosing, staging, and managing سرطانة العظم, guiding therapeutic decisions and prognostic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2447
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is classified as a form of اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (glomerular disease) and is frequently encountered as a component of متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome). As a severe, necrotizing inflammation of the renal glomeruli, it represents a specific subtype within the broader category of glomerular pathology. Its association with pulmonary‑renal syndrome highlights the clinical pattern in which simultaneous lung hemorrhage and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis occur, often due to small‑vessel vasculitis or autoimmune mechanisms. Recognizing necrotizing glomerulonephritis as both a distinct glomerular disease and a manifestation of pulmonary‑renal syndrome is essential for accurate diagnosis, prompt immunosuppressive treatment, and coordinated care of the intertwined renal and pulmonary complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Other and nonspecific abnormal cytological, histological, immunological and DNA test findings represent a broad category of atypical laboratory results, and within this category the specific finding تحليل الكروموسوم غير طبيعي (abnormal chromosome analysis) is included as a child or sub‑type, indicating that abnormal chromosome analysis is one of the nonspecific abnormal test results encompassed by this broader classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is a severe, necrotizing form of التهاب كبيبات الكلى (glomerulonephritis), representing a specific subtype within the broader category of glomerular inflammation. This disease is closely associated with متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome), a clinical entity in which acute lung involvement—often pulmonary hemorrhage—occurs together with rapid deterioration of renal function. Understanding that necrotizing glomerulonephritis both belongs to the spectrum of glomerulonephritis and can manifest as part of a pulmonary‑renal syndrome is essential for timely diagnosis, comprehensive evaluation of both kidney and lung pathology, and the implementation of appropriate immunosuppressive and supportive therapies to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2884
retrieved_docs.content: The neonatal disorder (اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة) includes as a specific manifestation the condition of fetal erythroblastosis (كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية), indicating that fetal erythroblastosis is a particular subtype or child entity within the broader category of neonatal disorders. Recognizing this relationship is important for accurate diagnosis and targeted management of affected newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 139
retrieved_docs.content: how specific autoimmune and hematologic diseases fit within broader disease families, identify pharmacologic options and contraindications for their management, connect advanced nuclear‑medicine diagnostic modalities, and clarify the hierarchical organization of symptoms and immune‑related disorders for comprehensive patient assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ and ربط البوق are medical concepts that frequently co‑occur, indicating that the presence of حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ is often associated with the finding or procedure ربط البوق. This consistent association suggests that clinicians should be aware of the link between these two entities, as recognizing one may prompt evaluation for the other, thereby informing diagnosis and guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 52
retrieved_docs.content: range of autoimmune hematologic and rheumatologic disorders are interconnected within the broader context of circulatory and connective‑tissue diseases, informing comprehensive patient evaluation and management.
